results.possibleIdentity: Manuel Rodriguez Sr. (1912–2017), often referred to as 'Mang Maning,' was a pioneering Filipino artist known as the 'Father of Philippine Printmaking.' He was highly active during the late 1960s and early 1970s, a period during which he significantly influenced the modern art scene in both the Philippines and New York. In 1960, he received a Rockefeller Foundation scholarship to study printmaking at the Pratt Graphic Center in New York. Between 1960 and 1962, he worked as a trainee in the print department of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). Rodriguez was known for his experimental approach to graphic arts, establishing the Contemporary Arts Gallery in 1962 and the Philippine Association of Printmakers in 1968. He moved to New York more permanently in the 1970s, where he continued to teach and create. His name appears in archives documenting the avant-garde scene of that era, including the 'Only One Larry Miller' archive, which contains photographs credited to Manuel Rodriguez that document Fluxus and performance art events associated with the circle of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and artists like Alison Knowles.

Manuel 'Spain' Rodriguez (1940–2012) was a prominent American underground cartoonist and artist active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Born in Buffalo, New York, he became a central figure in the countercultural underground comix movement. He is best known for creating the character 'Trashman' and for his contributions to the 'East Village Other' starting in the mid-1960s, as well as being a founding member of the United Cartoon Workers of America in 1970. While primarily known for his graphic narrative work and political activism, he was a key artistic figure in the same New York scene that birthed the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and other collaborative movements of the period.

results.possibleIdentity: Marc Cutler is a video artist and writer who was active in the early video art and technology movement during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is documented in the seminal video journal Radical Software (Volume II, Number 5, 1973) as an artist utilizing 1/2" portapak technology with a focus on exploring alternative urban environments. During this period, he was based in Scarsdale, New York, and was involved in a program at Antioch College related to the work of architect Paolo Soleri and his concepts of 'Arcology.' Cutler's work from this era is associated with the intersection of art, technology, and environmental design, which aligns with the mission of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). More recently, he has contributed scholarly texts to exhibition catalogs, including an essay titled 'The House Always Wins' for the 2021 Ericka Beckman exhibition 'Fair Game' and contributions to the 2019 catalog 'Where Art Might Happen: The Early Years of CalArts,' which examines the history of the California Institute of the Arts and its video pioneers. Additionally, a Marc Cutler from Nikon provided technical guidance and imagery for the 2018 launch of the Nikon Z7 camera, suggesting a long-standing expertise in imaging technology.

results.possibleIdentity: Josiah Edgerly (also known as Josiah W. Edgerly) was an artist active during the mid-to-late 20th century. He was an alumnus of the Skowhegan School of Painting and Sculpture, attending the program in 1961. This training placed him within a cohort of artists and faculty that included influential figures of the 1960s and 1970s. In the early 1970s and beyond, Edgerly appears to have been part of the same philanthropic and artistic circles as the founders of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.); notably, he and his associate Vivian E. Rea were listed as donors to the MDI Biological Laboratory in Maine alongside William F. Whitman Jr., the father of E.A.T. co-founder Robert Whitman. Later in life, Edgerly resided in Birch Harbor, Maine, where he served as a trustee for the Edgerly & Rea Revocable Trusts. He is noted as deceased in a 2024 institutional report.

results.possibleIdentity: Ken Marsh (also referred to as K. Marsh) is an American video artist, painter, and author who was a pioneering figure in the alternative video movement of the late 1960s and 1970s. He co-founded the People's Video Theater (PVT) in New York City in 1970 alongside Elliot Glass, focusing on community-based video journalism and the use of portable video technology (portapaks) to document social and political movements. In 1971, Marsh moved to Woodstock, New York, where he founded Woodstock Community Video (1972) and the Artists' TV Lab (1973), providing a space for artists like Gary Hill to experiment with electronic media. His work is often associated with the technological and collaborative spirit of the era, frequently appearing in chronologies alongside organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which promoted the intersection of art and engineering. Marsh is the author of several influential books on communications technology, including 'Independent Video' (1974) and 'The Way The New Technology Works' (1982). He has also maintained a lifelong practice as a painter and printmaker, with his work transitioning from early light shows to fine arts.

results.possibleIdentity: Ramon Serna was an engineer and technical collaborator who worked closely with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for his role as a systems engineer from the Ampex Corporation, where he collaborated with artist Robert Whitman. Serna was a key technical figure in the development of the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, a major E.A.T. project. He was responsible for the complex video and communication systems within the Pavilion, which featured pioneering uses of closed-circuit television and multi-channel audio. His work often blurred the lines between engineering and artistic contribution, a hallmark of the E.A.T. collaborations. Additionally, a 'Ramon Serna' is recorded as a participant or winning entrant in the 1968 E.A.T. competition for the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) exhibition 'The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age,' where he was recognized for a sculptural work that integrated technological components.

Ray Serna (also referred to as Ramon Serna) is a sculptor and artist who was active in the late 1960s and associated with the E.A.T. movement. He participated in the landmark 1968 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings: An Exhibition of Submitted Works Involving Technical Materials and Processes' organized by E.A.T. at the Brooklyn Museum. This exhibition showcased the results of the E.A.T. competition for collaborations between artists and engineers. Serna's work from this period typically involved kinetic elements, light, or industrial materials like plastic and electronics. He is sometimes identified in historical documents of the Chicano art movement as an early participant in experimental and technological art forms in California and the Southwest.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert O'Brien (born 1939) is a British-born artist and art educator who was a prominent figure in the Hong Kong art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. Born in the United Kingdom, he moved to Hong Kong and became recognized for his paintings and sketches. His work from this period is documented in the Asia Art Archive, including a catalog titled 'Robert O'Brien: Recent Paintings (1965–1974).' He was an active practitioner in the early modern art movement in Hong Kong, participating in key exhibitions such as 'Art' at the City Museum and Art Gallery (now the Hong Kong Museum of Art) between December 1969 and January 1970. He is also noted as a long-time art educator in the region.

Robert O'Brien (1954–1988) was a sculptor and installation artist who formed half of the influential artist duo Fortuyn/O'Brien, alongside Irene Fortuyn. Based in the Netherlands, the duo was known for creating 'sculptural environments' that merged art with functional spaces. While O'Brien became most recognized for his collaborative work in the early 1980s, his artistic development and works are represented in major collections such as the Kröller-Müller Museum, which holds pieces dating back to 1980. He died prematurely in 1988 at the height of his career.

Robert J. O'Brien (born 1954) is an American watercolorist who has been active as an artist for over fifty years, beginning his professional career in the early 1970s. He is a signature member of the American Watercolor Society (AWS) and the National Watercolor Society (NWS). O'Brien is known for his realistic landscape paintings, particularly of New England and Vermont, focusing on the interplay of light and shadow. Throughout his career, he has served as an instructor and participated in numerous national exhibitions, winning various awards including gold medals from regional watercolor societies.

results.possibleIdentity: Jay Burton (also known as Chester Jay Burton III) was a theatrical designer, lighting director, and educator active from the late 1960s. He earned a Master's degree in theater from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill in 1970, where his work focused on the staging of historical theater pageants. He later earned a doctorate from Florida State University. Burton is notably associated with the 'Found Space Theatres' and was a member of the United States Institute for Theatre Technology (USITT), an organization that shares significant professional overlap with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) due to its focus on the intersection of technology and performance. He served as the chairperson of the Speech and Drama Department at Alvin Community College for 44 years, from 1978 until his retirement in 2022. During his career, he worked as a designer and director for numerous productions, including experimental and community-based theater.

Jay Burton (1915–1993) was a prominent Emmy-winning comedy and variety show writer active from the late 1940s through the early 1990s. He was a prolific gag writer for television legends such as Bob Hope, Milton Berle, and Perry Como. His credits include 'The Hollywood Palace' (1964–1970), 'The Dean Martin Show', and 'The Dinah Shore Show', for which he won an Emmy for Outstanding Writing in 1956. While primarily known as a writer, he was active in the New York and Los Angeles television circles during the late 1960s and 1970s—a period when Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) was frequently collaborating with media professionals for its tech-based art events. His career is documented in various theatrical and television archives, including the Leland Hayward Papers at the New York Public Library.

results.possibleIdentity: Franco Ciarlo (born March 28, 1939) is an Italian-born painter and sculptor who has lived and worked in New York since 1967. He is notably recognized for his 'fresco transfer technique,' a process involving the application of fresco-like materials—including gauze, glue, and paper—onto large canvases. Born in Rome, Ciarlo studied at the Liceo Artistico and the University of Rome's School of Architecture before moving to the United States in 1964. During the late 1960s and 1970s, he became an active figure in the New York art scene, exhibiting at the Brooks Jackson Gallery Iolas and teaching at institutions such as Rockland Community College (1971) and the Pratt Institute (1975). His work, such as 'Structure Black, Orange, Purple,' is documented in the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) archives within the Dorothy C. Miller Papers, appearing in proximity to records for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Ciarlo's art is held in collections including the Everson Museum of Art and the private collection of David Rockefeller, Jr. In the 1980s, he was an active participant in the Plexus International art collective, contributing to multimedia performances and exhibitions like the 1987 'Art as Food' inquiry.
